---
title: "Sleep Hours vs Exam Scores Analysis"
format: html
---
Intuitively, you might expect that students who sleep more perform better on their exams.
However, the data is telling us a different story. The scatterplot below plots every student's
sleep hours and their exam score. The flat red trend line confirms there is
**little to no correlation** between how a student's sleep hours and how well they do
on  their exams. This tells us that sleep alone is not a meaningful predictor of academic performance.

```{r}
#| echo: false
#| message: false
#| warning: false

library(ggplot2)
library(plotly)

df <- read.csv("StudentPerformanceFactors.csv")

p <- ggplot(df, aes(x = Sleep_Hours, y = Exam_Score)) +
  geom_jitter(alpha = 0.08, color = "#3B5998", width = 0.25, height = 0.5, size = 1) +
  geom_smooth(aes(color = "Regression Line"), method = "lm", se = TRUE, linewidth = 1.2) +
  scale_color_manual(values = "red", name = "") +
  stat_summary(fun = mean, geom = "point", shape = 18, size = 4, color = "red") +
  scale_y_continuous(limits = c(40, 100), breaks = seq(40, 100, 20)) +
  scale_x_continuous(breaks = 4:10) +
  labs(
    title = "Sleep Hours vs. Exam Score",
    subtitle = "Red line = line of best fit (linear regression). Diamonds = group averages.",
    caption = "The nearly flat regression line indicates little to no relationship between sleep hours and exam scores.",
    x = "Sleep Hours per Night",
    y = "Exam Score (0–100)"
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 13)
pl <- ggplotly(p)
for (i in seq_along(pl$x$data)) {
  nm <- pl$x$data[[i]]$name
  if (!is.null(nm) && grepl("Regression Line", nm)) {
    pl$x$data[[i]]$visible <- "legendonly"
  }
}
pl
```
